--- SOURCE 3: The Impact of Poor Data Quality (and How to Fix It) - DATAVERSITY ---
URL: https://www.dataversity.net/the-impact-of-poor-data-quality-and-how-to-fix-it/

SUMMARY:
Poor data quality can lead to poor customer relations, inaccurate analytics, and bad decisions, harming business performance.

FULL CONTENT:
![](/wp-content/themes/dataversity/inc/images/dv-logo.png)

# Data Topics

# The Impact of Poor Data Quality (and How to Fix It)

![poor data quality](https://d3an9kf42ylj3p.cloudfront.net/uploads/2023/02/kf_poor_data-quality_march-23.png)

The dangers of poor data quality can cause significant damage to a business. Poor-quality data can lead to poor customer relations, inaccurate analytics, and bad decisions, harming business performance.

The sources of poor data quality may seem like a small issue, but the impact can easily become magnified as repeat errors and different types of errors increase and accumulate.

![](https://d3an9kf42ylj3p.cloudfront.net/uploads/2024/01/1x1.png)

![](https://d3an9kf42ylj3p.cloudfront.net/uploads/2024/01/1x1.png)

Missing or erroneous details in email communications can result in customers feeling insulted. Using research that contains errors in data will almost always lead to skewed conclusions. The combination of skewed conclusions and the accidental process of insulting the customer base does seem like a recipe for losing potential profits.

Good-quality data is not only helpful but is also necessary for managing projects, controlling finances, assessing performance, and delivering services efficiently. Although business leaders consider data quality important on a superficial level, they often treat it as a low priority. By giving data quality a high priority, the business can benefit from improved sales forecasts, more pleasant customer experiences, and better business intelligence.

[Business intelligence](https://www.dataversity.net/brief-history-business-intelligence/) is only as good as the data supporting it.

## **The Sources of Poor Data Quality**

The reasons for collecting and storing data of poor quality are pretty basic. Generally, the problems have to do with translating data from one format to another, but there are other sources, as well. The basic reasons for having poor-quality data are:

**Data integration issues:** Conversion errors can happen when data is collected from a variety of databases that [don’t integrate](https://rockcontent.com/blog/data-integration-challenges/) with the organization’s database. Converting one data format into another often leads to mistakes. CSV files, as a simple example, are typically separated by commas, and converting a spreadsheet to CSV files can result in data being stored in broken up chunks. Conversion issues can become even more complicated if the data taken from an older legacy system is converted for storage in a NoSQL system.

**Data-capturing inconsistencies:** [Data capture](https://theecmconsultant.com/what-is-data-capture/) is about taking the information stored on a document and transforming it into data for computer storage. This allows employees to retrieve, search, organize, and store documents quickly and efficiently. An organization having two or more departments that use different formatting processes should be concerned with data quality because of variations and inaccuracies. For example, one department may list the customer’s name as Rocal Ltd., while another lists it under the business owner’s name, Joe Jones.

**Poor data migration:** This commonly happens when data is moved from a legacy system to a new database, or to the cloud. [Moving data](https://www.premier-international.com/articles/7-reasons-data-migrations-fail) into a new system comes with some risks. Some of the data’s values can be missing or irregular. If the data isn’t of good quality upfront, new problems can arise, such as data corruption and missing data.

**Data decay:** Data decay describes the deterioration of data quality, typically in the marketing and sales departments. [Data decay](https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2021/09/20/data-decay/) is often an expression of old, outdated information. (For example, approximately 40% of email users will change their email addresses every two years. Has your organization developed a response to that issue?) Some have referred to a database crash as a form of data decay.

**Data duplication:** Duplicated data may skew business intelligence. It is possible for problems to develop if duplicated data is used for statistical purposes. Also, if [duplicated data](https://profisee.com/blog/8-business-process-problems-that-result-from-data-duplication/) is correct in one location, and missing parts in another, problems can arise.

## **The Consequences of Poor-Quality** Data

The consequences of using data of poor quality can range from minor to disastrous. It can result in lost income, employees quitting in frustration, and even painful monetary fines. There are several potential consequences:

**A loss of income:** Losing clients and potential clients will have a negative impact on revenue. In addition, [Gartner](https://www.gartner.com/smarterwithgartner/how-to-stop-data-quality-undermining-your-business) recently discovered many businesses lose thousands of dollars each year as a result of “lost productivity” stemming from poor-quality data. Poor data quality costs organizations an average of $15 million per year.

Poor-quality data can cause lost income both directly and indirectly:

**Inaccurate analytics:** [Data analysis](https://www.dataversity.net/developing-a-data-analytics-strategy/) or predictive analytics, when based on incomplete and inaccurate data, risks misinterpretation and poor decision-making. Duplicated data, missing fields, etc., can produce an analysis that should not be trusted.

**Fines for privacy invasions:** [Europe](https://gdpr-info.eu/), [California](https://oag.ca.gov/privacy/ccpa), [Canada](https://www.priv.gc.ca/en/privacy-topics/privacy-laws-in-canada/the-personal-information-protection-and-electronic-documents-act-pipeda/), and [Br](https://lgpd-brazil.info/)azil have made significant efforts to protect the privacy of their citizens. The United States has tried to protect its citizens’ medical information. If a business collects personal data and uses it “illegally” (things become illegal when organizations and people ignore ethics in favor of profits), the business can expect a [hefty fine](https://www.csoonline.com/article/3410278/the-biggest-data-breach-fines-penalties-and-settlements-so-far.html). Other issues resulting in fines include making people’s personal data available to criminals by using minimal, poor, or no security, not notifying authorities (or customers) of data breaches affecting customers, and collecting personal data on children.

**Reduced efficiency:** The majority of internal business processes require reliable data to function optimally. If the business’s data is incomplete or inaccurate, then staff will have to spend time researching information to correct the error, and correcting it manually. The time it takes to make these corrections drags down business’s efficiency and profitability. This can be especially damaging to a business if some of its departments use a [data silo](https://www.dataversity.net/a-brief-history-of-data-silos/) philosophy (making corrections difficult).

**Missed opportunities:** Without high-quality data to base your decisions on, your business will miss important opportunities. For example, poor data may mean you miss out on:

Inaccurate data can also reduce lead generations, making it more difficult to target potential prospects.

**Reputational damage:** This often goes hand in hand with lost income and damages the business’ growth. Customers will [quickly lose trust](https://statuslabs.com/what-is-reputational-damage/) in a business if they believe it is mishandling their personal data. (Mishandling personal data can also result in fines.) Customers often share bad experiences online in an effort to warn others of a dangerous situation. Issues that might raise red flags for customers are:

**Wasted time, wasted money:** Cleaning up data after it has been stored is much more time-consuming than using standardized behaviors to collect and process data. Businesses can easily lose profits if their sales teams waste time with bad leads that have been created using low-quality data. Marketing departments can launch expensive marketing campaigns that fail because the wrong demographics.

**The destruction of morale:** Low-quality data can destroy the morale of sales and marketing teams. They can waste hours and hours searching for new opportunities based on faulty data. If sales staff (or researchers) have to manually identify and correct poor data – time-consuming and tedious – it can damage faith in the organization’s leadership.

## **Three Basic Methods for Obtaining High-Quality Data**

For a business to operate at its best, it needs high-quality data. This helps in making informed, intelligent decisions, and increases efficiency to maximize profits. You can use these three basic methods to improve data quality:

1. Develop a workplace culture that supports the gathering and storage of high-quality data. The culture must support intelligent data rules designed to standardize data formats and eliminate data duplication. Poor-quality data is usually the result of a lack of standardized guidelines and procedures. Implementing rules for handling data helps to ensure high-quality data. Some useful guidelines are:

2. Audit and clean the data. It’s important to check the quality of a business’s data on a regular basis. Conducting a data audit reveals problems. Continuous, real-time data maintenance eliminates the use of stagnant and decayed data, and ensures the marketing and sales teams are working with useful data.

# Data Quality: The Impact of Poor Data Quality

by [Scott W. Ambler](https://ScottAmbler.com)

[Data quality](https://agiledata.org/essays/dataquality.html "Data Quality (DQ) in an Agile World") is a measure of how well your data meets or exceeds the criteria set for it. Unfortunately, many organizations suffer from poor quality data, what many refer to as [data debt](https://agiledata.org/essays/datatechnicaldebt.html "Data Technical Debt: Addressing Data Quality Problems") or simply data debt.

## Why Should You Be Concerned About Poor Data Quality?

## How Does Poor Data Quality Impact Your Organization?

Data technical debt is a serious problem for several reasons:

The quality of your data has a material impact on your organization. This is why the Agile Data (AD) method advises you to be “[quality infected](https://agiledata.org/essays/philosophies.html#QualityInfected)” and to ensure the high data quality within your organization.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Poor data quality fosters doubt among researchers, jeopardizing the trust needed for collaboration and further exploration, as inaccurate findings cannot be reliably reproduced.
Inaccurate data leads to erroneous conclusions, skewing research outcomes and potentially resulting in misguided policies and practices, ultimately impacting public perception and health.
Ineffective data compromises the success of citizen science initiatives, reducing their impact and wasting resources, which can discourage future participation and funding.
